claims.claim: Lithium-ion batteries (NMC811/SiC, 21700 and 18650 formats) reached venting gas temperatures exceeding 1370°C during thermal runaway, with surface temperatures surpassing 400°C and venting gas velocities exceeding 150 m/s.
claims.quote: Results indicate that the lithium-ion batteries reach venting gas temperatures exceeding 1370 °C, with surface temperatures surpassing 400 °C and venting gas velocities exceeding 150 m s−1.

claims.claim: Sodium-ion batteries (NaMn1/3Fe1/3Ni1/3O2 cathode, 18650 format) exhibited lower temperatures than lithium-ion batteries due to the absence of gas ignition during thermal runaway, with peak venting gas temperatures not exceeding 350°C and all surface temperatures remaining below 400°C.
claims.quote: Conversely, the investigated sodium-ion batteries exhibit lower temperatures due to the absence of gas ignition, precluding the estimation of venting gas velocity... the measured cell surface and venting gas temperatures remained under 400°C and no gas ignition was observed in the tested cells.

claims.claim: Peak mass flow rates during thermal runaway reached 2000 g/s for 18650 LIBs and 1500 g/s for 21700 LIBs, but lasted only several milliseconds, while average mass flow rates ranged between 30 g/s and 300 g/s.
claims.quote: Peak mass flow rates for lithium-ion batteries reach 2000 g s−1, with average rates between 30 g s−1 and 300 g s−1... The peak values of the mass flow rate were even higher - 1500 g s−1 for 21 700 LIBs and 2000 g s−1 for 18 650 LIBs but lasting only several milliseconds.

claims.claim: Total mass loss during thermal runaway ranged from 55% for 18650 sodium-ion batteries to 77% for 18650 lithium-ion batteries, with the lower mass loss in SIBs attributed to lower temperatures, absence of ignition, and lower energy content.
claims.quote: Total mass loss ranges from 55 % in 18650 sodium-ion batteries to 77 % in 18650 lithium-ion batteries... The investigated SIBs have a lower nominal capacity and, consequently, lower stored energy leading to less ejection during TR. No ignition of SIBs was observed in the high-speed videos, which also contributes to the lower mass loss of SIBs.

claims.claim: The TR start temperature of 18650 LIBs ranged between 190°C and 200°C, while the TR start temperature of investigated SIBs was on average 10°C to 15°C higher, indicating greater thermal stability of the investigated SIBs.
claims.quote: The TR start temperature of the 18 650 LIBs ranges between 190°C and 200°C determined based on the cell surface temperature 𝑇surface mid1, with some observed fluctuations between tests... Compared to LIBs, the TR start temperature of investigated SIBs is, on average, 10°C to 15°C higher, and, thus, the TR occurred later despite the same heating rate, indicating that investigated SIBs could be more thermally stable compared to investigated LIBs.

claims.claim: The venting gas velocity of LIBs in both 21700 and 18650 formats exceeded 100 m/s at the beginning of thermal runaway and exhibited exponential decay, falling below 50 m/s after 200 ms and below 25 m/s after 500 ms from the start of the optically visible TR event.
claims.quote: Both LIB formats show similar trends of venting gas velocity over time, exhibiting an exponential decay, as represented in Fig. 4. The high velocities exceeding 100 m s−1 at the beginning of TR can be attributed to the gas ignition. The venting gas velocity decreases after 200 ms, falling below 50 m s−1 in both LIB formats and further drops below the 25 m s−1 value after 500 ms since the beginning of the optically visible TR event.

claims.claim: A lower heating rate combined with a higher SOC leads to greater total mass loss of lithium-ion cells during thermal runaway, due to more homogeneous temperature distribution and simultaneous TR across all cell areas.
claims.quote: Willstrand et al. [6] demonstrated that the lower the heating rate and the higher the SOC level, the greater the total mass loss of the cell. The low heating rate is responsible for a homogeneous temperature distribution within the cell. Consequently, it may lead to simultaneous TR across all cell areas and higher mass loss.

claims.claim: The absence of gas ignition in investigated sodium-ion batteries may be primarily related to the cathode material (NaMn1/3Fe1/3Ni1/3O2), which has better thermal stability than the cathode material of the investigated lithium-ion batteries.
claims.quote: Therefore, the lack of gas ignition in investigated SIBs may be related primarily to the cathode material, which has better thermal stability than the cathode material of LIBs.

claims.claim: The mass flow rate for 18650 SIBs could not be estimated due to the absence of gas ignition and the consequent unavailability of venting gas velocity values.
claims.quote: Notably, the mass flow rate for the 18 650 SIBs could not be estimated due to the absence of ignition and the venting gas velocity values.

claims.claim: The transition time between safety valve opening and thermal runaway event under continuous 128 W heating was on average 128 s for 21700 LIBs, 115 s for 18650 LIBs, and 154 s for 18650 SIBs.
claims.quote: The transition time between the safety valve opening and the TR event with continuous heating at 128 W is, on average, 128 s for 21 700 LIBs, 115 s for 18 650 LIBs and 154 s for 18 650 SIBs.

claims.claim: Thermal runaway did not occur in NTM SIBs at any of the five SOCs tested (0%, 60%, 80%, 90%, 100%), whereas thermal runaway occurred in LFP LIBs at 60%, 80%, 90%, and 100% SOC but not at 0% SOC.
claims.quote: The maximum temperature rise rates (Rmax) of the NTM SIBs at five SOCs and the LFP LIB at 0 % SOC was not more than 1 ◦C/s during the heating, which indicated that the TR do not occur. However, the Rmax of the LFP LIBs at 60 %, 80 %, 90 % and 100 % SOCs was more than 1 ◦C/s and last for more than 3 s, meaning that the LFP LIBs at those SOCs experienced TR.

claims.claim: The safety valve opening temperature (Topen) of LFP LIBs was approximately 30% higher than that of NTM SIBs under the same SOC, indicating that the material inside the SIB was less thermally stable.
claims.quote: the values of Topen in the LFP LIBs were 120.4 ◦C, 118.9 ◦C, 119.0 ◦C, 116.5 ◦C, 120.0 ◦C for 0%–100% SOCs, respectively which was approximately 30 % higher than that of SIB under the same SOC. This further indicated that the material inside the SIB was less thermally stable.

claims.claim: The maximum temperature (Tmax) and maximum temperature rise rate (Rmax) of LFP LIBs were 491.4°C and 48.9°C/s respectively, far exceeding those of NTM SIBs at 167.9°C and 0.6°C/s.
claims.quote: The Tmax and Rmax of the LFP LIBs were 491.4 ◦C and 48.9 ◦C/s, respectively, which were far more than that (167.9 ◦C and 0.6 ◦C/s) of the NTM SIBs

claims.claim: At 100% SOC, the FED value for LFP LIBs (75.70186) was 28.90 times higher than that for NTM SIBs (2.53196), indicating significantly greater asphyxiation hazard from LFP LIB vent gases.
claims.quote: At 100 % SOC, the FED values of NTM SIBs and LFP LIBs were 2.53196 and 75.70186, respectively. The value for LFP LIBs is 28.90 times higher than that for NTM SIBs.

claims.claim: The maximum mass loss rate in LFP LIBs was approximately 2–7 times larger than that of NTM SIBs at the same SOC, with LFP LIB mass loss rates increasing from 11 g/s to 18 g/s as SOC increased from 0% to 100%.
claims.quote: the maximum mass loss rate in the LFP LIBs was about 2–7 times larger than that of the NTM SIBs at the same SOC, which was related to the gas release rate of the batteries. The maximum mass loss rates of the NTM SIBs were 4 g/s, 3 g/s, 2 g/s, 2 g/s, and 4 g/s while the that the LFP LIBs were 11 g/s, 13 g/s, 16 g/s, 17 g/s, and 18 g/s of from 0 % to 100 % SOC, respectively.

claims.claim: For NTM SIBs, CO is the first parameter to respond and is suitable as an early warning indicator of thermal hazard, whereas for LFP LIBs, voltage responds first and is suitable for early warning.
claims.quote: For the NTM SIB, the response order of the characteristic parameters is CO > H2 > voltage > CO2 > HF > surface temperature > EX. For the LFP LIB, the response order of characteristic parameters is voltage > surface temperature > HF > EX > CO2 > H2 > CO. Thus, for early warning parameters of thermal hazards, CO can be selected for NTM SIBs while voltage could serve for LFP LIBs.

claims.claim: The dominant gas emitted by NTM SIBs was CO2, whereas H2 accounted for the majority of released gases from LFP LIBs, with H2 in LFP LIBs reaching 5412 ppm at 100% SOC compared to a maximum of 92 ppm in NTM SIBs.
claims.quote: the dominant gas was CO2 for NTM SIB while H2 accounted for the majority of the released gases for LFP LIB... the maximum concentrations of H2 surged from 219 ppm to 5412 ppm as SOC increased, which was far larger than that for the NTM SIBs.

claims.claim: CO concentration in LFP LIBs at 60%, 80%, 90%, and 100% SOC exceeded the lethal threshold of 447 ppm, whereas CO concentrations in NTM SIBs at all SOCs (maximum 80 ppm at 100% SOC) remained significantly below the lethal threshold.
claims.quote: The maximum CO concentrations from 0 % to 100 % SOC were 48 ppm, 50 ppm, 51 ppm, 75 ppm, and 80 ppm, respectively, all of which were significantly lower than the lethal threshold... The maximum CO concentrations increased dramatically from 140 ppm to 3306 ppm, representing a 2261.43 % rise. The CO concentration at 60 %, 80 %, 90 % and 100 % SOCs were all significantly higher than the lethal threshold.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of a 280Ah LFP battery, the primary ejected gases are H2 (55.22%), CO2 (23.17%), and CO (7.97%), with a single cell releasing an average of 6.6301 mol of gas.
claims.quote: the primary gases ejected during thermal runaway of 280Ah LiFePO4 batteries are H2 (55.22 %), CO2 (23.17 %), and CO (7.97 %), with a single cell releasing 6.6301 mol of gas

claims.claim: The mass ratio of non-condensable gas to condensable liquid (electrolyte) ejected during thermal runaway of a 280Ah LFP battery is approximately 1:10, with ejected gas accounting for ~2% of total battery mass and condensed electrolyte liquid accounting for ~20%.
claims.quote: the mass of the ejected gas accounted for about 2 % of the total battery mass, the wreckage accounted for about 78.1 %, and the condensed electrolyte liquid accounted for about 19.9 %. This indicates that electrolyte condensation behavior cannot be ignored in energy storage systems. The flow of 1080.9 g electrolyte within the battery pack may induce arcing hazards. Furthermore, the mass ratio of gas to liquid in the ejected substances was approximately 1:10.

claims.claim: During thermal runaway of two batteries in a battery pack, the pressure balance valve opened 36 times, with 72.2% of pressure relief events occurring at a rate of 0.0–0.5 kPa/s, and the maximum pressure reduction rate of 1.5–2.0 kPa/s occurring only once.
claims.quote: the pressure balance valve opened 36 times. The pressure threshold for the first opening differed from subsequent openings... Among these, the 0.0–0.5 kPa/s rate was the most frequent, accounting for 72.2 % of the total activations in this experiment.

claims.claim: The first opening of the pressure balance valve during battery pack thermal runaway occurred at approximately 107.69–107.23 kPa, while subsequent openings occurred at approximately 105.74–106.24 kPa, both slightly above the manufacturer-specified opening pressure of 104.825 kPa.
claims.quote: the first opening occurred at approximately 107.69–107.23 kPa, while subsequent openings occurred at around 105.74–106.24 kPa. The manufacturer-specified opening pressure for the pressure balance valve was 104.825 kPa, and the experimental results were slightly higher than this reference value.

claims.claim: The four corners inside the battery pack were identified as sites where thermal runaway airflow and electrolyte aerosols accumulated, evidenced by black ablation marks, requiring enhanced electrical insulation protection.
claims.quote: It is speculated that the TR airflow accumulated in the four corners, resulting in the formation of these black marks. Therefore, when designing battery modules, additional insulation protection should be provided for the batteries located at the edges and corners.
